coredata.dc:title: Covalent organic polymer modified TiO2 nanosheets as highly efficient photocatalysts for hydrogen generation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Qingqing
Peng, Peng
Xiang, Zhonghua
coredata.dc:description: Splitting water into hydrogen driven by solar energy is considered as one of the major strategies to provide clean energy. TiO2-based photo-catalysts have attracted everlasting interests due to low cost sources and excellent stability. However, the wide band gap and limited quantum efficiency of TiO2 hinder its application. Herein, a novel photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution was initially prepared by modification of TiO2 nanosheets with covalent organic polymer (i.e., COP-64). The COP-64 not only can provide more reaction active sites by increasing the specific surface area of the pristine TiO2 nanosheets, but also improve light harvesting in visible light region. Moreover, the suitable energy level of COP-64 provides charge transfer channels and favors the electron transfer, which greatly inhibits the combination of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) in the hybrid system. Particularly, the corresponding hydrogen production rate of the hybrids reaches 15.02mmolh−1 g−1, which exceeds 63% compared with that of pristine TiO2 nanosheets (9.20mmolh−1 g−1).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic polymers
Water splitting
Hydrogen evolution
Covalent organic frameworks
Hydrogen energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by NSF of China (51502012; 21676020); Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2162032); The Start-up fund for talent introduction of Beijing University of Chemical Technology; Talent cultivation of State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites; The Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (ZY1508), and The “111” project of China (B14004). We are thankful to Dr. Yufei Zhao from Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry (TIPC), Chinese Academy of Science (CAS) for helpful discussion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthetic route for COP-64 using the nickel-catalyzed Yamamoto-type Ullmann cross-coupling reaction. TBP=1,3,6,8-tetrabromopyrene, TBB=1,3,5-tris(4-bromophenyl) benzene. To clearly show, we used white and green spheres to represent carbon from TBP and TBB, respectively. Yellow spheres represent Br and hydrogen atoms are omitted. The theoretical atomistic network for COP-64 is statistically shown and the real structure may be more complicated. (B) SEM and (C) TEM image of as-synthesized COP-64. (D) XRD spectra of pristine TiO2 (i.e., TC0), COP-64 and the hybrid (i.e., TC1). The peaks in COP-64 spectrum are consistent with the monomer TBP because the rigid polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from pyrene can form similar crystalline structure as monomer even after polymerization (He et al., 2000; Li et al., 2014). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The TEM images (A) and the sectional TEM image (B) of TC0; HRTEM images (C) and (D) of TC1.
The survey XPS spectra of the COP-64 (A) and TC1 (B); the fitted C 1s (C) and O 1s (D) XPS.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (TC0 and TC1) and the corresponding pore-size distribution (inset).

UV-Vis absorption spectra of the as-synthesized hybrids and pristine TiO2 and COP-64. The COP-64 incorporation extends the photo adsorption to the visible light region (particular in 400–550nm) of pristine TiO2.

(A) Comparison of the photocatalytic activity of the samples for the photocatalytic H2 production from methanol aqueous solution under simulated sunlight and (B) typical time course of H2 production from water catalyzed by TC1.
Time-resolved transient photoluminescence (PL) decay of TC0 (A) and TC1 (B).
Cyclic voltammograms of the COP-64 between −2.5 and 1.5V.
Schematic illustration for the charge transfer and separation in the COP-64-modified TiO2 nanosheets system under solar light irradiation.
Effects of COP-64 on porous properties of TiO2.

head.ce:title: Covalent organic polymer modified TiO2 nanosheets as highly efficient photocatalysts for hydrogen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Splitting water into hydrogen driven by solar energy is considered as one of the major strategies to provide clean energy. TiO2-based photo-catalysts have attracted everlasting interests due to low cost sources and excellent stability. However, the wide band gap and limited quantum efficiency of TiO2 hinder its application. Herein, a novel photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution was initially prepared by modification of TiO2 nanosheets with covalent organic polymer (i.e., COP-64). The COP-64 not only can provide more reaction active sites by increasing the specific surface area of the pristine TiO2 nanosheets, but also improve light harvesting in visible light region. Moreover, the suitable energy level of COP-64 provides charge transfer channels and favors the electron transfer, which greatly inhibits the combination of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) in the hybrid system. Particularly, the corresponding hydrogen production rate of the hybrids reaches 15.02mmolh−1 g−1, which exceeds 63% compared with that of pristine TiO2 nanosheets (9.20mmolh−1 g−1).

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of science and technology has caused energy shortages and environmental pollution, water splitting driven by solar energy therefore has been considered as one of the major strategies to solve these problems (Wang et al., 2015). Since Fujishima and Honda found the photoelectrocatalytic water splitting on TiO2 electrode in 1972, much effort has been focused on TiO2 photocatalyst due to low cost and excellent stability for water splitting (Fujishima and Honda, 1972). However, the applications of TiO2 photocatalysts are still impeded due to the major bottleneck drawbacks, e.g., wide band gap. With the band gap as wide as 3.2eV, anatase TiO2 absorbs light only in the UV region, which only accounts for 4–5% of the full spectrum (Colmenares et al., 2016). Moreover, the recombination of photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) reduces the quantum efficiency of TiO2 (Zhou et al., 2014). Up to now, many effort has been made to solve the above problems, such as heteroatom doping (Serpone and Emeline, 2012; Pan et al., 2015; Xiao et al., 2016; Di et al., 2010), multicomponent hybridization (Osterloh, 2013), fabrication of alternative one-dimensional (1D) nanostructures (Osterloh, 2013; Yang et al., 2009) and introducing heterojunction (Di et al., 2010).

Recently, metal-free carbon materials have been widely employed to modify TiO2 photocatalysts because of high charge mobility along with cheap, abundant, stable and non-toxic properties. Yan and Yang (2011) reported the preparation of TiO2–g-C3N4 composite materials with verified ratio. The coupling of TiO2 and C3N4 can effectively promote the separation of photo-generated e−-h+ pairs, subsequently enhance the absorbance band intensity of TiO2–g-C3N4 and H2 evolution rate. Yu et al. (2010) synthesized the graphene-modified TiO2 nanosheets with exposed (001) facets (graphene/TiO2) through microwave-hydrothermal treatment. When the optimal graphene content was 1.0wt%, the corresponding hydrogen production rate exceeded more than 41 times compared with pristine TiO2 nanosheets, which can reach 736mmolh−1 g−1. Moya et al. (2015) demonstrated a route towards hierarchical assemblies of mesoporous TiO2 and carbon nanotube (CNT) by a combination of electrospinning and sol–gel methods. The results showed that 10vol% of CNT doping can enhance the photo activity with an order of magnitude compared with pure TiO2, reaching hydrogen evolution rates of 1218mmolh−1 in the presence of sacrificial reagents under UV irradiation. However, most of these metal-free carbon materials still can improve the photo activity in UV region, rare in visible light region, for TiO2, which leads to the limited utilization for full spectrum. Therefore, it is a challenge to obtain TiO2 hybrids with highly efficient photoinduced property for the further visible light driven photocatalysis.

Covalent organic polymers (COPs) are a new class of porous network materials, which are composed of non-metal elements and connected by strong covalent bonds (BO, CC, CH, CN, etc.) (Wan et al., 2015; Khachatryan et al., 2015; Özdemir et al., 2015; Xiang and Cao, 2013; Xiang et al., 2015, 2016). COPs have attracted more and more extensive attention in recent years due to their large conjugated system, large specific surface area (1000–6000m2 g−1), tailorability and high hydrothermal stability (Xiang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015). Therefore, COPs can be considered as a new class of photocatalyst (Chen et al., 2014; Guo et al., 2013; Stegbauer et al., 2014). Among the developed COPs, COP-64 shows high sensitivity and luminescent property with wide light absorption in both UV and visible light region (Guo and Cao, 2015). By virtue of these properties, COP-64 holds the promise to fully utilize the solar source by combining with TiO2 to form heterostructured photocatalysts. Moreover, the incorporation of COP-64 can also enhance the specific surface area of photocatalyst because of its high porosity. Herein, we prepared a series of TiO2@COP-64 hybrids and studied their photocatalytic performance. The H2 production rate of the optimal hybrid (i.e., TC1) achieved as high as 15.02mmolh−1 g−1, which was 1.63 times higher than that of the pure TiO2 nanosheets.
All reagents were purchased from commercial sources (Alfa Aesar, Sigma Aldrich, analytical grade) and used as received.

The COP-64 sample was synthesized according to previous reported method (Guo and Cao, 2015). Typically, 1,5-cyclooctadiene (cod, 0.505mL, 3.96mmol) was added into a dry dimethylformamide (DMF, 60mL) solution of bis(1,5-cyclooctadiene)nickel(0) ([Ni(cod)2], 1.125g, 4.09mmol) and 2,2′-bipyridyl (0.640g, 4.09mmol) under magnetic stirring until completely dissolved and a purple solution was formed. After that 1,3,6,8-tetra-bromopyrene (0.325g, 0.628mmol) and 1,3,5-tris((4-bromophenyl)-ethynyl) benzene (0.085g, 0.157mmol) were added to the above purple solution, respectively. The whole system was heated at 85°C under an argon atmosphere for 12h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the deep-purple suspension was filtered out while washed by concentrated HCl. Followed by further purification with CHCl3 (5×15mL), tetrahydrofuran (5×15mL), and H2O (5×15mL) respectively, the obtained products was dried in vacuum oven to get COP-64.

TiO2 nanosheets were prepared with a modified hydrothermal method based on previous studies (Yu et al., 2010). Typically, 25mL of Ti(OC4H9)4(TBOT) and 3mL of hydrofluoric acid solution were mixed in a 100mL dried Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 24h to form a white suspension. After hydrothermal reaction, 100mL of 0.1M NaOH aqueous solution was added to neutralize the hydrofluoric acid. The white precipitate was collected by centrifuge, washed with distilled water and ethanol for three times, and then dried in an oven at 80°C for 6h, leading to the formation of F-free TiO2 nanosheets.

A determined amount (0, 8, 10 and 30mg for TC0, TC0.8, TC1 and TC3, respectively) of COP-64 particles were added into 60mL glycol under sonication and kept for an hour to form a homogeneous suspension. Then 1g of TiO2 nanosheets were added and stirred for 2h. The whole system was transferred to a 100mL dried Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 6h. The resulting solution was filtered with distilled water for three times and dried in an oven at 80°C for 12h, resulting in TiO2@COP-64 hybrids (remarked as TCX, X represents the mass ratio of COP-64/TiO2).

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-prepared samples were determined by a X-ray diffractometer (SHIMADZU XRD-6000) using CuKα radiation source (λ=1.54056Å) at 50kV and 200mA in the 2θ range from 5° to 70°. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) studies were taken on a JEOL JEM-2100F and JEOL JEM-3010 microscope equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) analyzer, respectively. A double beam UV-visible spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje TU-1901) was used to record the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra in the region of 260–800nm with BaSO4 used as a reflectance standard. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted by a VG Scientific photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) using 300W AlKα radiation. The base pressure was about 3×10−9 mbar. Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy was recorded at room temperature by Lifetime and Steady State Spectrometer (FLS 980). N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were measured at 77K with a Micromeritics ASAP 2020. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation was used to calculate the apparent surface area from adsorption data obtained at P/P 0 between 0.05 and 0.3. The total volume of the mesopores was calculated from the amount of nitrogen adsorbed at P/P 0 =0.99, referring to the total pore volume including the surface condensation. Pore size distribution data were calculated from the N2 sorption isotherms based on the BJH model in the Micromeritics ASAP 2020 software package (assuming slit pore geometry). Ultra-high-purity grade He (99.999%) and N2 (99.9992%) were used for all adsorption measurements.

Reactions were carried out in a 145mL closed Pyrex quartz bottle at ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure. H2 production was performed by dispersing 0.02g of photocatalyst powders in an 80mL aqueous solution containing methanol (10vol%) as sacrificial electron donors. Pt (3wt%) was photodeposited on the photocatalyst as co-catalyst by dissolving H2PtCl6 into the reactant solution and illuminating for 30min at room temperature. Nitrogen was bubbled into the suspension for about 20min to remove air. The irradiation was taken place under a 300W Xe-lamp (Microsolar300) without a UV-cutoff filter (20cm away from the photocatalytic reactor). 0.5mL gas was extracted out every 0.5h through the septum to analyze the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate using a gas chromatograph (GC-7890B, Agilent, TCD, nitrogen as a carrier gas, and 5Å molecular sieve column).

The schematic representation of synthesis of COP-64 is shown in Fig.1 A and detailed characterization except morphology can be found in the previous reference (Guo and Cao, 2015). The TEM image shows that COP-64 exhibits layer texture in short range (Figs.1C and 2D) and sphere morphology in long range (Fig.1B). Since the photocatalytic performance of TC1 is the best among the as-synthesized hybrids (vide infra), the structural characterizations for the hybrids were focused on TC1. The structure of the as-synthesized products was determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements. Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of COP-64, TC0 (pure TiO2 nanosheets), and TC1, respectively. Clearly, only anatase phases of TiO2 (space group: I41/amd, JCPDS No. 21-1272) (Yu et al., 2010) was observed in the as-synthesized pure TiO2 nanosheets. All the diffraction peaks in TC1 match well with the pure TiO2 nanosheets and none characteristic peaks of COP-64 was observed, suggesting that the incorporation of COP materials do not change the crystal structure of TiO2.

Fig.2 A and B shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the TC0 samples, i.e., TiO2. A batch of nanosheets were developed through the modified hydrothermal method and formed well-defined sheet-shaped structures. The products have a rectangular texture with an average side length of ca. 40–60nm and thickness of ca. 5nm. The TC1 samples, i.e., COP-64 modified TiO2, have similar micromorphology as pristine TiO2. The three lattice spacings of 0.189, 0.237 and 0.337nm correspond to the characteristic lattice planes for the (200), (004) and (204) of TiO2 in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) of TC1 (Fig.2C). The amorphous texture on the outside of TiO2 nanosheets in Fig.2D can be ascribed to the present of COP-64. Therefore, it is valid to infer the successful incorporation of COP-64 in TiO2 hybrids along with the XPS results spectra of COP-64 and TC1 (vide infra).

To further investigate the combination between COP-64 and TiO2 nanosheets, the chemical state and existing interactions between the components in composites are confirmed by high-resolution XPS analysis. As illustrated in Fig.3 A and B, the obvious peaks of C and O in the survey spectrum of COP-64 and TC1 can be clearly detected, indicating the existence of carbon and oxygen elements in the samples. Compared with COP-64, the survey spectrum of TC1 also shows the presence of Ti2p originating from TiO2, as shown in the inset of Fig.3B. The inset exhibits two major peaks with binding energies at 458.5 and 464.2eV, corresponding to the distinct Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 signals in the Ti4+ chemical state (Song et al., 2016). As shown in Fig.3C, the two high resolution XPS data of C1 peaks of COP-64 are detected at 284.8eV (CC) and 285.9eV (CO), which suggest the presence of C1s in COP-64 and the physically absorbed oxygen or water molecules respectively due to high surface area along with the following high resolution O1s (vide infra; Xiang et al., 2014). For the TC1 composite, the main C1s signal at 284.6eV can be ascribed to the CC and CC. The sharp signal detected at 285.6eV indicates existence of the absorbed oxygen or water molecules in the hybrid (Xiang et al., 2014). Meanwhile, the new peak detected at 288.3eV indicate the formation of TiOC structures after hydrothermal reaction (Wang et al., 2012, 2016) which reveals the success of combination between TiO2 nanosheets and the COP strucutre. Therefore, it is valid to infer that the carbon in conjugated COP-64 system can be connected with TiO2 via COTi bonds along with O1s results (vide infra). In addition, the XPS results of O 1s are consistent with the above analysis results, as shown in Fig.3D. As mentioned above, the O 1s peak of COP-64 at 532.5eV is attributed to physically absorbed oxygen or water molecules. Compared with COP-64, there is a clearly O 1s signal of TC1 composite located at 529.7eV, which can be assigned to TiOC (Li et al., 2016a,b). And the peak of absorbed oxygen or water molecules also slightly changed to 531.4eV because the formation of TiOC structures. All these results further suggest the strong interaction between the Ti, O, and C after hydrothermal treatment, suggesting the successful of combination between COP-64 and TiO2 nanosheets.

Generally, the physical size and structure of catalyst also play an important role in photocatalytic reaction. We also employed N2 adsorption measurements to evaluate the porosities of the hybrids. Fig. 4 and Table 1 show the pore structure, BET surface areas and the related pore-size distributions (the inset in Fig. 4) of TC0 and TC1. The N2 adsorption isotherms for TC0 and TC1 show type IV according to IUPAC classification (Miao et al., 2016) and reveal hysteresis loops at relative pressures close to unity, which indicates the formation of large mesopores and macropores. Such organized porous structures possess efficient transport pathways for the reactant and product molecules, and benefit for photocatalysis. The formed mesopores and macropores are derived from the aggregation of TiO2 nanosheets because the single crystal nanosheets show nonporous texture (Zhu et al., 2014). The pore size distributions of TC0 (Yu et al., 2010) and TC1 (inset in Fig. 4) samples are almost in the range from 5 to 100nm, which matches well with the above conclusion. Table 1 lists the textural parameters analyzed by adsorption–desorption isotherms data. COP-64 has a large specific surface area as high as 1158m2 g−1, indicating the existence of porous organic skeleton. Comparing with TC0 samples, the TC1 has larger specific surface area (154m2 g−1), smaller average pore size (12.4nm) and pore volume (0.47cm3 g−1). The combination of COP-64 with TiO2 nanosheets effectively increase the specific surface area and subsequently improve the photocatalytic performance because a large surface area can provide more reactive sites.

Furthermore, the effect of COP-64 on the optical properties was investigated through UV-vis absorption spectra which converted from the corresponding diffuse reflectance (DR) spectra by means of the Kubelka-Munk function. Fig. 5 shows that the COP-64 presents a larger absorption region with an intense transition in visible light region, which is beneficial for light harvesting (Zang et al., 2009). For pure TiO2 nanosheets, there is a strong light response within the UV region (λ<400nm), related to the electron transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB). On the contrary, pure TiO2 nanosheets show a weak ability of transition response in the range of 400–800nm. After introduction of COP-64, a series of hybrids (TCX, X=0.8, 1, 3) display the same absorption edge as TC0 in the UV region. The results indicate that COP-64 was only the substrate for immobilization rather than incorporated to the lattice of pure TiO2 nanosheets. However, there is an obviously red shift in the absorption edge and a stronger broad background absorption with increasing COP-64 content, which can be ascribed to the optical absorption of COP-64 in visible light region These results prove that COP-64 can improve the utilization ratio of visible light, therefore promote the efficiency of the titanium dioxide and generate better photocatalytic activity.

In order to evaluate the hydrogen producing performance of the photocatalyst, we carried out a series of measurements. Fig.6 A shows the comparison of the photocatalytic H2-production activity of TC0, TC0.8, TC1 and TC3 from 145mL of 20vol% methanol aqueous solution containing 0.02g of samples. Methanol is used as sacrificial agent to remove photo-generated holes and 3wt% Pt is used as co-catalyst. Cleary, the COP-64 has no photocatalytic activity for water splitting. The H2 evolution of TC0 is only 9.2mmolg−1 h−1. This may result from the rapid recombination of CB electrons and VB holes in pure TiO2 and the presence of a large over potential in the production of H2. The H2 evolution of the composites increases with the increasing content of the COP-64 and all shows higher evolution result than TC0, indicating that the COP-64 has a significant influence on the photocatalytic activity of pure TiO2 nanosheets. As the mass fraction of the COP-64 reaches 1%, TC1 displays the highest H2 production corresponding to 15.02mmolg−1 h−1, which is over 1.6 times than that of TC0. However, further increase of the COP-64 concentration causes the decline of photocatalytic activity. Since pristine COP-64 has no photocatalytic activity for water splitting, the further increase of amount of COP-64 in the hybrids may reduce the energy utilization of TiO2 nanosheets, which reduces photogenerated e− and h+ for producing hydrogen and leads to the decline of photocatalytic activity.

Furthermore, the regeneration of TC1 was carried out to further evaluate the stability of the photocatalysts. Fig.6B shows the time dependence of the photocatalytic H2 production. After five consecutive operations, the photoactivity shows pretty good durability, indicating sufficient stability of this hybrids for H2 evolution.

To further reveal the charge transfer path and light catalytic reaction mechanism in TC1 hybrid, the fluorescence decay curves with 385nm excitation light were measured to reveal the recombination dynamics of photogenerated e− and h+ for both TC0 and TC1 samples. These kinetics were fitted to multi exponential decay functions and quantify the hole-transfer rates well, The results were listed in the insert in Fig.7 A and B, respectively (Jun et al., 2013; Nagy et al., 2012). Among these short-lived lifetimes, 0.058 and 0.153ns are the major components (97.79% and 78.57%) for the TC0 and TC1 respectively and long-lived lifetimes 3.954 and 1.360ns are the corresponding minor components (2.21% and 17.83%). Remarkably, the TC1 also has the third component (3.6%) relating to the lifetimes 9.011ns, which may be caused by the interaction of COP-64 and TiO2. The inset in Fig.7B displays that after loading the COP-64 material, the average lifetime (4.76ns) of TC1 composite becomes longer than the TC0 (2.42ns), suggesting the emergence of a pathway from the electron transfer between COP-64 and TiO2 nanosheets (Li et al., 2016a,b; Zhang et al., 2016). The COP-64 effectively suppressed the recombination of the photogenerated electron and hole. The longer life time of the separated electron-hole benefits the photocatalytic reactions (Kavery et al., 2015).

The energy band was also calculated to understand the mechanism for enhancement of COP-64. Fig. 8 reveals the electrochemical properties of the COP-64 sample by using CV measurements in a three-electrode cell setup with 0.1M C16H36BF4N as the supporting electrolyte (Ye et al., 2011). The sample was deaerated by bubbling with pure nitrogen gas for 30min at room temperature. The oxidation potential is located at −0.92V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and the related HOMO energy levels of COP-64 materials is −3.46eV (vs. Ag/AgCl). Together with the reduction potential as 1.02eV (shown in Scheme 1 ), the related LUMO energy levels was calculated as −5.3eV by the following equation:
HOMO = - e ( Eox + 4.38 )
LUMO = - e ( Ered + 4.38 )

The data shows that the both conduction band (CB) and the valence band (VB) positions of COP-64 are higher than those of TiO2 (Kudo and Miseki, 2009). According to the above results, the H2-production process of the COP-64 modified TiO2 hybrid under the solar light can be understood as shown in Scheme 1 (Cui et al., 2012). When the light irradiates on the catalyst, the VB electrons (e+) of TiO2 are excited to CB, generating holes (h+) in the VB as well as COP-64 due to the fluorescence. Normally, after charging separation and migration of photogenerated carriers to the surface of TiO2, these charge carriers will recombine in a short time, then a fraction of the electrons participate in the photocatalytic reaction and the corresponding holes is captured by the sacrificial agent, resulting in low reactivity. However, the modification of COP-64 can effectively avoid this phenomenon. When the carriers migrate to the surface of TiO2@COP-64 hybrids, the photogenerated e+ will transfer from the CB of COP-64 to the CB of TiO2, which increases the amount of e+ for photocatalytic reaction and produce H2 more efficiently. In addition, some of the photogenerated h+ in the VB of TiO2 is captured by the sacrificial agent while some move to COP-64. The transfer process of photogenerated h+ can further promote the separation of photoinduced h+ and e+, which also increases the amount of e+ to some extent. In this way, the effective charge transfer at the interface between the COP-64 and TiO2 nanosheets not only enhances the amount of e+, but also inhibits the combination of photoinduced h+ and e+, resulting in the enhanced photocatalytic activity.

In summary, the COP-64 modified TiO2 nanosheets, i.e., TC1 hybrids, was first successfully prepared by hydrothermal method for photocatalytic hydrogen production. Our experimental results show that the highly porous COP-64 not only can provide more reaction active sites by increasing the specific surface area of the pristine TiO2 nanosheets, but also improve the light harvesting in visible light region. Additionally, the interface between TiO2 nanosheets and COP-64 provides a charge transfer channels and favors the electron transfer from conduction band of COP-64 to that of TiO2 and the hole transfer from the valence band of TiO2 to that of COP-64. Hence the modification of COP-64 can effectively inhibit the combination of photogenerated e+ and h+ in the hybrid system. Particularly, the corresponding hydrogen production rate of the hybrids reaches 15.02mmolh−1 g−1, which surpasses 63% compared with pristine TiO2 nanosheets (9.20mmolh−1 g−1). Accordingly, covalent organic polymers can be considered as a new class of efficient photovoltaic materials with promising potential for photocatalytic water splitting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSF of China (51502012; 21676020); Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2162032); The Start-up fund for talent introduction of Beijing University of Chemical Technology; Talent cultivation of State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites; The Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (ZY1508), and The “111” project of China (B14004). We are thankful to Dr. Yufei Zhao from Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry (TIPC), Chinese Academy of Science (CAS) for helpful discussion.
